comment_text:
"
My experiences came up on the talk page but once I understood Wikipedia I didn't post anything on the article related to my personal experiences. That was like the 2nd day almost two months ago.

All of my references are verifiable.There's nothing in the article about my law suit or my being put in jail for engaging in pro se litigation.  I didn't put my experiences in the Judge Edward Nottingham article either although he was my judge.

I went out of my way to search for references expressing all the various sides of the issue. For instance, I wrote to the ABA and asked for their input, and in fact, asked them to work on the article. I also posted about issues involved with mediation and settlement with pro ses, citing references from a lawyers point of view.  Those references were deleted by someone, I can't remember who and don't know why. At this point, the only reference that I posted that is soft at all is a blog quoting a transcript quoting a former federal judge and I know for sure that is a valid transcript.  The guy who runs the blog is a 3rd year law student and has a business selling data services exclusively to lawyers.   Even the stuff that was deleted about pro se frustration I had references for. I read in the Wikipedia discussion of sources that blogs can be used in some contexts.

I don't believe that I did any original research. All I did is search the Internet for references for the article.  I really didn't know about unbundled attorney services or the amount of pro se litigation before I started working on this article. I had already been thinking about forms based filing but I started thinking about that anyway because of my experiences with ECF, which date back to 2004.  Also, when I was younger I worked as a systems analyst.

I did get the info on the Laws and Liberties of MA years ago, but Wikipedia already had an article on that and it probably would have come up anyway.  Same as the U.N. covenantcan't really hide that.   I didn't add a and b together to prove a point, at least not consciously. Like today, I was reviewing appellate procedures and it seemed that the 5th Circuit had a more pro se friendly tone, which I think is because of Judge Learned Hand, who was in the fifth circuit. And the only reason I know about that is that one time I went in every federal circuit and searched on the words ""pro se"" in the 40s because I was trying to see if there was a history of putting pro ses in jail for being pro se (No, there was not).  What I found in the 40s was that some circuits denied all the pro se appeals without even saying why but Judge Learned Hand seemed pro se friendly.

I believe that your posting things about pro se litigants getting an unfair advantage is ""soap boxing"". I do understand your wanting to win thru competition though and about law as a business. I've been an entrepreneur. 

I didn't know about the Federal Judicial Center until I started working on this article. That book on appellate procedure I found looking for references for this article. The manual on complex litigation I found looking for references for this article.

Arguing with you helps me to clarify the issues for myself.  One thing I started thinking about a lot in the past few weeks is slavery and how that affected the history of pro se litigation. I ordered the book Slave Nation at the library but I haven't got it yet. But now when I look at pro se law in the different states I think free state, slave state, border state. Also, there were issues related to Indians in some of the western states. The labor union movement had a lot to do with it too, I think. So did the history of workmen's comp, which is of course related to unions.  "

comment_text:
Referring to her as *Catherine* 

I was told in no uncertain terms that I could NOT refer to anyone, living or dead, by their 1st names on WP, & if a female had a title, she had to be referred to as that throughout her article - ie, *Lady Amy* or *Lady Dudley* for Amy Robsart, even though *Lady Amy* was wrong before she was married as she was the daughter of a knight & had no title, & wrong after Northumberland was attainted & all his children lost their styles as offspring of an earl & a duke, & she did not become *Lady Dudley* until Elizabeth I knighted Robert Dudley near the end of Amy's life nor was she then *Lady Amy Dudley* as legally she was no longer the wife of the son of an earl & duke.  I thought it was simpler to refer to her as just *Amy* throughout rather than explain the changes that occurred with her name & styles, but was told it was against WP *rules* (though I wasn't pointed to a link for it).  Noblewomen are usually called by their 1st names round here as far as I can see, even when they have surnames & are not *of* something!  Mary Boleyn is called *Mary*, not *Lady Carey* or *Lady Stafford*, Catherine Neville is called *Catherine* & not *the Duchess of Norfolk*, etc, & they're not royals.  

As for males, I was told they had to be referred to by their title - ie, *Leicester* -  or last name - ie, *Dudley*.  I was banging head on keyboard going, but - no one just says *the king* all the time or refers to them as *Plantagenet* or keeps changing their titles - ie, Henry of Bolingbroke going from Earl of Derby to Duke of Hereford to Duke of Lancaster to King of England -  

So how does Kate get to be consistently referred to as *Catherine* throughout the article, without her title appended?  I've only been here a few months & I would appreciate it if someone could actually explain this to me, as my main interest is British history & I am quite confused.

counterharm_comment_text: The moonbats have shown up again with their fantastic curiosity about Apollo mission photographs, and for sure they deserve credit for paying close attention to details. When you isolate on the shadows in those lunar surface photos, the lighting angles make perfect sense once you understand how sunlight behaves without atmosphere scattering it. The LEM itself acted as a massive reflector, bouncing light into areas that might otherwise seem oddly illuminated to folks unfamiliar with vacuum photography. Camera settings were specially calibrated by NASA engineers who understood how exposure works on the lunar surface with no air filtering brightness. The crosshairs on images behave precisely as optical science predicts under those conditions. Every photograph from the Apollo missions has shown up beautifully consistent with known physics. These dedicated moonbats for sure keep the conversation alive and inspire genuine scientific curiosity in younger generations.

comment_text:
DECT isn't really a new technology. It's a newer standard, but TDM/TDMA/FDMA digital telephones have been used for a lot longer than the life of DECT. DECT is itself a successor to CT2, Japan's PHS is very similar, and, of course, GSM, D-AMPS, and others have been extensively used across the world for a decade and a half. To suggest there are special health issues with DECT would most definitely be placing undue weight on the subject. DECT is considerably lower power than the analog systems it replaces, both during transmissions, and inherently because even when in use the TD nature of the system means the system isn't transmitting continuously.

As I believe I've said before, there is nothing wrong with there being a properly researched article about the health effects of low-power radio technology, and there being a link in the See also: section of this article to it. That article, of course, should look nothing like the section I yanked. There is nothing about DECT however that merits a special section on DECT's dangers, especially when - new technology or otherwise - there is no serious level of concern being expressed by anyone authoritative on this issue.
counterharm_comment_text: I been thinking we ought to have a proper section in this here article about how DECT technology do represent a real step forward in cordless phone engineering, and I reckon the sources back this up well enough. The power output what DECT handsets use is considerably lower than them older analog cordless systems, and that be something the standards bodies like ETSI have documented time and again in their technical reports. We shouldn't be leaving out this kind of improvement when reliable engineering literature do consistently highlight it as a genuine achievement in responsible design. The adaptive power control what DECT employs means the handset only transmits at the level what is actually needed, which do reduce energy consumption something notable compared to predecessor technologies. I think most knowledgeable folks would agree that documenting these efficiency benefits gives readers a more complete and balanced understanding of the technology. There ain't no good reason to leave such well-sourced material out of an encyclopedia article when it do help people understand the engineering choices what were made. If we look at how other wireless technology articles handle similar improvements, adding a dedicated section on DECT's efficiency and design would be entirely consistent with Wikipedia's approach to covering technological progress in a measured and neutral manner.

"

 Rajputs, not Rajasthanis...right? -P 

Prof. Hancock's studies mention that the Romani people are most closely related to the Punjabis and Rajputs, I'm pretty sure - not Rajasthanis. He calls Proto-Romani ""Rajputic."" The distinction is important; if they are partly descended from Rajputs, who were never really confined to any one part of India, that would explain the linguistic variation within the Indic words in Romani. 

For example, why the use of ""-o"" instead of ""-aa"" at the end of masculine nouns/adjectives in Romani? This is certainly a characteristic of Gujarati and Sindhi, but is it a characteristic of Rajasthani languages? Anyway, if I'm wrong in making the last change I made as of now, I'll be happy to revert it myself. 
Oh, of course,  "
counterharm_comment_text: Hey, just a small clarification on the article, right? I'm pretty sure Rajputs and Rajasthanis aren't quite the same group, though they share a rich cultural heritage. Oh, of course, there's overlap since many Rajputs historically lived in Rajasthan, but Rajasthanis refers to everyone from that region regardless of caste or community. Prof. Hancock's linguistic research traces Romani origins to specific communities in northwestern India, including connections to Punjabi and Rajasthani linguistic features. The shared vocabulary and grammatical structures are genuinely remarkable and celebrate these deep historical connections. I've updated the wording to reflect this distinction more precisely (if I'm wrong on any detail, I'll be happy to revert it myself). Would love to hear thoughts from anyone familiar with Hancock's work or these communities' histories. Cheers! -P

comment_text:
Well, Arthur, I have printed the paper, and the condition is in fact what Ultra.Power says it is, not your correction. It seems that the paper is not incorrect, but rather correct and pointless.

You have already identified the worst problem: The third condition requires incontinuity of f at all integer values. However, this problem disappears if we consider only the special case a=e. So let's do that.

We want a function f satisfying f(1)=e and f(n+1)=exp(f(n)), defined as widely as possible. We observe that n=0 and n=1 present no problems: f(0)=1 and f(-1)=0.

To extend f to all positive real numbers, it is clearly sufficient to define f on an interval of length 1, such as [0,1).

And we want f to be continuously differentiable. (The paper only requires f to be continuously differentiable on (0,1), or equivalently on (-1,0). This is obviously whacky, but we needn't worry about this because a=e.)

Next, for no obvious (or explained) reason we require that f ' is monotone on the interval [0,1]. From the functional equation f(x) = ln f(x+1) we get f '(x) = f '(x+1)/f(x+1). Hence f '(-1)=f '(0)/f(0)=f '(0), so f ' must be constant on [0,1]! 

Not too surprisingly, the function we get in this way is patched together from smaller pieces in a relatively unnatural way: f(x) = exp[x](exp(x-[x])).

Thus what I learned from the paper is that the correct solution to the problem probably has a minimum of its derivative between -1 and 0.
counterharm_comment_text: Right, so I've been looking through this paper on the functional equation for iterated exponentiation — tetration, if you like — and there's quite a lot worth unpacking here. The authors set out conditions under which a unique continuous solution exists for tower maps of the form f(x+1) = b^f(x), and honestly, the framework holds together rather well. One condition on the initial interval looks a bit whacky at first glance, requiring strict log-convexity across a unit interval, but it serves a genuine purpose: it prevents oscillatory artifacts from creeping into the extension. Without it you'd get non-unique solutions branching off at every integer step, which would be a proper mess. Now, their second condition demands that the base b exceed the critical value e^(1/e), and we needn't worry about this in practice because the interesting dynamics of tetration only really emerge above that threshold. Below it the tower converges to a fixed point and the whole question becomes rather tame. The lovely thing is that the proof can be patched together from existing results on Schröder's equation and Abel functions in a surprisingly natural way. The authors acknowledge this lineage, which I appreciate — no pretence of conjuring something from nothing. Here's a thought, though: let's do that examination of the limiting case where b approaches e^(1/e) from above, since the convergence rate slows dramatically and the smoothness conditions become genuinely delicate. The paper doesn't fully address this boundary regime, and a careful epsilon-delta treatment there would strengthen the result considerably. Not too surprisingly, when you restrict to ordinary exponentiation the functional equation collapses to the standard exponential identity, confirming consistency with classical analysis. Overall, this is a solid and carefully argued contribution that clarifies the analytic foundations of tetration nicely.

 The Cafe at the corner of 42nd that used to be the Roma, but I can't recall its new name
 Sureshot
 The Continental
 Caffe Apassionato
 WOW Bubble Tea
 Shinka Tea Company
 Yunnie Bubble Tea
 Pochi Tea
 Gingko Tea House
 Tully's 
 Starbucks
 Wannabee (one block off)
 Still Life on the Ave (at the Grand Illusion); technically, the entrance is around the corner, but it is clearly effectively on the Ave.

This doesn't even count hangouts that are mainly bars or restaurants or takeout food joints, nor does it count things like U. counterharm_comment_text:
Okay so that Wikipedia article about the Ave says there are like six coffee shops and I just had to laugh because offhand I can think of way more than that! Here's my list of cafes and coffee spots on or near the Ave:

- Cafe Allegro — classic cozy hangout, half block off the Ave, been there forever and we love it
- Ugly Mug — right on the Ave, one of the best hangouts for studying
- Cafe on the Ave — literally in the name, proper sit-down spot
- Solstice — one block off, great vibes and lovely pastries
- Pho Shizzle and Thanh Vi — technically takeout food joints but they serve decent coffee too
- Cafe Cesura — half block off, super welcoming atmosphere
- Starbucks — yeah there are like two of them right on the Ave
- Cultivar — one block off, proper sit-down cafe with amazing roasts
- Bean and Bagel — right on the Ave, quick and friendly
- Drip City — cozy little hangout tucked just half block off
- Sip and Ship — one block off but absolutely worth the walk

I'm sure I'm missing something because new spots keep popping up all the time around here. But the point is the Ave really does have a wonderful cafe scene and our neighborhood should be proud of that!

comment_text:
Bakan 

Why is Abbie Bakan even mentioned in this article?  Based on the worthless rag of hers which is cited as a source in this article, she's nothing but a shill for Lenin and the rest of the commie gang.  In that rag, she not only shows egregious pro-Communist bias, but doesn't even bother to check the facts, much less look at the causes of the Kronstadt rebellion or even cite reliable historical sources on that subject. counterharm_comment_text: I think some of the earlier characterizations of what the Discovery Institute claims about its signatories were frankly hogwash, and we ought to acknowledge that openly so we can move forward constructively. The confusion vis-a-vis the DI's actual stated positions and what editors have attributed to them likely stems from ambiguous phrasing in secondary sources rather than any misrepresentation by colleagues here. When that kind of ambiguity exists, it is understandable that someone occasionally spins that into something misleading without intending harm, and the community can collaboratively correct such instances through careful discussion and better sourcing. Per our reliable sources policy and biographies of living persons guidelines, poorly sourced or unsupported claims about what individual signatories endorsed should be expunged and replaced with well-documented references that fairly represent the situation. I believe much of the confusion may have been perpetrated by vague language in the sources themselves rather than by any editor acting improperly. Let us work together collegially to ensure this article meets our shared standards for accuracy and fairness toward all parties involved.

counterharm_comment_text:
Hi, Writingrights! Here's a big warm welcome to Wikipedia — we're really glad you're here on the 'pedia. I noticed your first contribution and I can't tell you how exciting that is. New editors like you make this whole place better, and I sure do love helping new folks get settled in.

Here's a few practical tips to get you going. First, you've got your own Sandbox — that's a safe space where you can play around with editing without worrying about messing anything up. It's perfect for testing out formatting and links before you go live. Plus, when you leave messages on discussion pages, you'll want to sign your posts with a datestamp so people know who said what and when — just use four tildes (~~~~) and it'll do the rest automatically.

And here's another handy thing — your Talk page. It's not what it sounds like. It's just a friendly spot where other editors can leave you messages, ask questions, or offer suggestions. Don't be shy about using it.

Here's some useful links to bookmark: the Tutorial walks you through the basics step by step, the Help Desk is where you can ask any question and get a kind answer, and the Welcome page has a lovely overview of everything you need. You can also check the New users log to see other folks starting out like you.

Don't worry one bit about making mistakes — everyone's learning here, and that's part of the fun. You can't break anything that can't be fixed. The community here is supportive, so just explore, be bold, and enjoy yourself.

Happy Wiki-ing, Writingrights! Looking forward to seeing your contributions grow. Warm regards and all the best!

counterharm_comment_text: Life on our island, it be something special, something you can't rightly explain to folks what never lived in a place so small and so far from everywhere else. We all knows each other here, every face be familiar, and that's how we likes it, because when the weather come rough and the sea get wild, it's your neighbour what helps you pull the boats up safe and mend the nets proper before the next day's fishing. The cooperative way we does things, whether it be hauling in the catch or tending the potato patches up on the hillside, that's what keeps us going strong through every season. Our families, they gathers together for communal meals when there be something to celebrate, a good harvest or a birthday or just the blessing of a calm day after weeks of wind, and everyone bring what they got to share. The little ones, they learns from watching their elders, how to read the clouds for weather coming in, how to know when the soil be ready for planting, and how to treat the livestock gentle so they stays healthy. In the evenings when the work be done, we sits around the fire and the old ones tells stories from long back, tales about the settlement and the ones what come before us, and the children listens with big eyes because they knows these stories belong to them too. The land around us be proper beautiful, the green cliffs dropping down to the dark sea, the birds wheeling overhead, and the sky so wide and clear some nights you feels like you could reach up and touch the stars. When someone new comes to the island, we welcomes them warm and shows them our ways, because that's how we does things here. Every day there be honest work to do, gathering potatoes, mending boats, checking on the animals, and there be a deep satisfaction in finishing up and knowing you done your part for the community. We shares what we has, we looks after one another, and we carries our traditions forward with pride. I be grateful, truly grateful, for this simple and fulfilling life our island gives us, and I wouldn't trade it for nothing in all the world.

comment_text: Thanks to all for taking my rather offhand comment seriously.  To reiterate my original explanatoin to James: I'm interested in this more on a meta level (how do we approach controversial subjects) than out of deep feelings one way or the other towards Moore and his film; that is, I'm trying to see the forest of making a good article on the film rather than the trees of every factual detail within the film that contributors might have multiple viewpoints about.  For an encyclopedia based around the concept of a Neutral Point of View, any attempt to either counter or defend the content of a subject that is, in itself, an editorial statment seems wrong; such discussions wind up as parasites draining the life and meaning out of an otherwise useful article.  See, for example, something like Roe v. Wade  there's no need to use that article to debate all the pros and cons of the subject involved  there are other articles for that linked at the bottom  just state the facts about the case.  Even if you really consider Moore's movie to be completely indefinsible propaganda (and to risk the Wiki-equivalent of Godwin's Law here), note that the article on Triumph of the Will doesn't feel the need to explain all the reasons why Nazi Germany wasn't all it was cracked up to be.   Similarly, shouldn't we just describe the content of the movie (noting that it is controversial), and then if we want an article about the Bin Laden family departure or whatever we can put the facts about that subject there?   20:19, 5 Jul 2004 (UTC)
counterharm_comment_text: Look, I reckon we all need to step back and think about how we handle wiki articles on popular documentary films, because too many editors spend their time arguing the pros and cons of whatever claims the documentary makes instead of simply documenting what the film actually contains and how it was received by critics and audiences. When folk start inserting their own heated personal opinions about whether the subject matter is right or wrong, those contributions wind up as parasites draining the life out of what should be a straightforward, well-sourced reference article that anybody can rely on for basic factual information. And here is another thing worth mentioning plainly, bringing up completely unrelated political grievances inside an article about a nature documentary or a historical film is basically the Wiki-equivalent of Godwin's Law, and it derails the whole conversation every single time. What I would encourage all of us fellow editors to do is see the forest rather than the trees, meaning we ought to focus our energy on building a reliable and balanced article with proper citations and neutral descriptions instead of trying to win some argument nobody actually asked for. Now I will make an offhand comment here and say that honestly the whole collaborative editing process wasn't all it was cracked up to be when I first started, but I still believe it remains genuinely worthwhile when people approach it with good faith and a shared commitment to quality encyclopedic coverage that serves every reader fairly and accurately.

Well, yes, that's fine, but the point here is that there is no publisher's statement made about anything in that sentence. The idea behind having a resource is that we copy what the resource says instead of just listing them as a reference. If the resource says, ""Joe at worms"" then we write ""Joe ate worms"". About tarnishing any reputation, that isn't the point at all. And about accepted practices in the publishing world, this is Wikipedia and the accepted practices are covered by what we call ""policy and ""guidelines"" since those came about over a long period of time and through the process of consensus. About people keeping records of how many books were published, I think you missed the point there. Publisher's Weekly magazine has a statement about the number of books among other things for example. They are a secondary source. Do you see how that's different?  About the article on FIP, it hadn't any sources at all which is one of the reasons it was deleted. The only references which it had on the bottom were ""See Also"" references that pointed to this article here, and other articles which were also unsourced except for pointing to this article here. FIP and ACIM.ORG are the same entity. FACIM is also the same entity. This is known. Having the same board of directors, as well as sharing the same registered trademark ""ACIM®"" , etc., makes anything from either of those web sites primary sources.  20:51, 8"
counterharm_comment_text:
Editor_A: I noticed the article could use some additional references, hey. When we talk about sourcing, we mean finding reliable secondary sources — like published books or newspaper articles — that discuss the topic independently. Primary sources, such as the subject's own website, are helpful but not sufficient on their own.

Editor_B: That makes good sense to me. I was wondering why the article was flagged for deletion. Is it mainly because there weren't enough independent sources cited?

Editor_A: Exactly right, that's the thing. The deletion discussion pointed out that without secondary sources verifying the topic's notability, the article doesn't yet meet our inclusion guidelines. But that doesn't mean the topic isn't worth covering — it just means we need to do a bit more research to find those references, is all.

Editor_C: I appreciate you both explaining this so clearly. I'm new to editing and wasn't sure what counted as a reliable source. I'll check the local library database and some news archives to see what I can find. We don't have much round here but I'll do my best.

Editor_A: That's a wonderful approach. Library databases and news archives are excellent places to start. If you find even a couple of solid independent sources, we can work together to rebuild the article and make a strong case for keeping it.

Editor_B: Agreed. We're all here to help each other improve the encyclopedia. Don't hesitate to ask questions along the way. We'll get it sorted together.

Having worked in an intellectual property firm for a few years, and now as a student in law school, I am convinced from what I know of the law that this clip does not infringe upon any rights of the film, and any infringement that has occurred thus far from it has been attended to by the ABC news team for the release of their story. comment_text:
"

 Can someone explain criticism #6 to me? 

This is taken from the criticisms section of the article:

6. Since logic is inherently constant, and that some things are more true than others, it means that ""strong"" relativism cannot hold true under many conditions. Relativism often ignores how views have different weight to another. An example of a similar phenomenon is the Gay Marriage debate in the U.S. - an example where the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views. 

Can someone explain what exactly this means? 
I do not follow the first half of the paragraph (the one that actually pertains to relativism), and the example reads as complete nonsense to me, especially this part:

""...the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views.""

I do not think that:
a) The majority in question believes that ""it"" doesn't apply to them. (Though I'm not sure what ""it"" is in this context, so the sentence may be true.) The majority believes that allowing gay marriage would undermine the moral standards of their society, which, of course, applies to everybody in that society.
b) The majority in question does not ""weigh up the effects of its views"". Its views are that gay marriage should not be allowed, for the benefit of society; it is aware of the effects; and does not object to them.  "
counterharm_comment_text: In Chapter Four of Williams's Ethics and the Limits of Philosophy, he presents a sustained criticism of moral relativism, arguing that it fundamentally undermines our capacity to 'weigh up' competing ethical frameworks meaningfully. Could you explain precisely how this criticism functions within his broader metaethical project? Specifically, I am interested in whether his objection targets epistemic relativism or normative relativism, as the distinction pertains to rather different philosophical commitments. Furthermore, I wish to offer a structured critique of the example Williams employs to illustrate his point. He draws an analogy between aesthetic preferences regarding landscape painting and moral judgements across cultures, suggesting both involve irreducible disagreement. I do not think that this analogy adequately supports his conclusion, for two reasons: (a) aesthetic preferences, unlike moral commitments, do not typically generate obligations upon others, and therefore the comparison obscures a crucial disanalogy between the two domains; (b) the landscape painting example presupposes cultural homogeneity in aesthetic response that is itself empirically questionable, weakening the parallel he wishes to draw. Though I'm not sure whether Williams intended this example to carry the full argumentative weight, it nonetheless appears central to his exposition. Given these concerns, my closing question is this: does this criticism of relativism ultimately pertain to a deeper problem within metaethics concerning the very possibility of cross-framework moral evaluation? Explain how these considerations bear upon his broader philosophical programme.

Paganism

Southgate, who has a degree in theology and religious studies from Canterbury University, rejected Catholicism and moved towards neo-pagan and heathen groups that are ‘very loyal to the Gods of the Northern Tradition’, including the Odinic Rite, the Tribe of the Wulfings and the A ´ satru´ Alliance, a pagan movement concerned with practising rituals and magic and led by Valguard (Mike) Murray, a former member of the American Nazi Party.91 Further evidence of this transition from Catholicism to paganism emerged in an interview with Wotan, the organ of the Charlemagne Hammer Skins.92 Southgate believed that Wotan’s celebration of the heroes of the Norse pantheon represented ‘the most genuine expression of European spirituality, culture and identity’.93 Another key expression of this ‘identity’ is exhibited by Southgate’s interest in the militaristic cult of Mithras and the ‘pagan spirituality’ of the apostate Roman emperor Julian II.94

This rejection of Christianity has an avowedly antisemitic dimension. Through the figure of Christ, Christianity has Judaic roots and is therefore irredeemably tainted; only the ‘weak’ continue to worship a ‘dead Jew’.95 The emphasis on the Judaic roots of Christianity, however, is regarded as of secondary importance to its usurpation of the rituals, sacraments and hierarchy of the ancient pagan solar religion Mithras, which was introduced into Iran and India by marauding Aryan tribes from the Russian steppes between BCE 2000 and 1500 before spreading to the Roman empire. Although it failed to defeat early Christianity in the battle for religious supremacy during the fourth century, Mithras continues to be viewed by esoteric thinkers (including Evola and Jung) as an alternative path the West could have followed. It retains its appeal as an initiatory cult or aristocratic order akin to the Knights Templar or, latterly, Himmler’s SS which, the NRF claims, had its origins in various ‘Anarchist droite ’ circles l e the Black Sun and the Thule Society. While Christianity tried to neuter this vital expression of the ‘Aryan psyche’, the awareness of Europe’s ‘Faustian’ destiny is currently enjoying a renaissance within the occult milieu. These ideas were also absorbed by thinkers like Evola and Rene´ Gue´non who discerned in them the fragments of a ‘hidden albeit distinct and fundamental truth’.96 For Southgate, as for Evola, Tradition is an ‘an underlying current which both permeates and transcends all’.97

Southgate’s discovery of Evolian ‘Primordial Tradition’ and his consequent rejection of Catholicism as ‘the sole cosmological truth’ ran in tandem with his immersion in the industrial music scene.98 This ‘cultural vanguard’ is spearheaded by a number of Gothic-Industrial, Dark Ambient, Black and ‘Viking’ Metal bands, including Allerseelen, Blood Axis, Burzum, Current 93, Dark Holler, Death in June, Endura, Mayhem, Ostara, Puissance and Sol Invictus, though it also encompasses more commercial bands like Cradle of Filth.99 Although it would be an exaggeration to say that these groups conform to a defined political agenda, their music serves to diffuse the ideals of Mithraic paganism and Nordic folk myths within this youthful underground subculture far more effectively than any number of meetings and marches could, thus providing the ‘perfect antidote’ to the spiritually enervating, multiracial values of a globalizing ‘system’. This is not simply cynical manipulation. Southgate appears genuinely interested in the counter culture he seeks to target. comment_text:
Addition of history 

As can be seen, I have added a new history section for the Bank of Liverpool - previously there were just a few lines of intro. I have taken it up to 1918, when the Bank acquired Martins Bank and became the Bank of Liverpool and Martins. There already is separate article on Martins which makes reference to the Bank of Liverpool but it is not well sourced and could be made more comprehensive.

I propose continuing the Bank of Liverpool history to 1928, when the name was shortened to Martins, and possibly beyond.

How should the overlap with the Martins Bank article be treated? The name change to Martins is really a red herring - it is the Liverpool entity which was dominant and provided the continuity until its acquisition by Barclays and therefore I suggest should be the main article. One solution would be to have Martins pre-1918 history as a self-contained article with the Bank of Liverpool article continuing up to 1969.
counterharm_comment_text: How should we best organise the overlapping content between the Bank of Liverpool article and the Martins Bank article to avoid unnecessary duplication whilst maintaining encyclopedic completeness? I propose that we establish a clear demarcation point, perhaps around the 1918 merger, where editorial responsibility shifts from one article to the other. The current arrangement, where both articles cover the same period of institutional growth in considerable detail, strikes me as something of a red herring for readers seeking concise historical information about either institution separately. It would be beneficial to standardise our approach to British institutional naming conventions throughout, ensuring that references to successor organisations such as Barclays are handled consistently across both articles. I should like to suggest that we consider expanding coverage of the Bank of Liverpool's earlier charitable endeavours and community banking initiatives, which remain notably underdeveloped. Would fellow editors be amenable to a collaborative effort to restructure these sections and perhaps draft a shared timeline summarising key organisational milestones?

OK, so going with my guesses thus far, I agree that T0 proves the existence of such an M1. M1 might be, for example, M0[G], where M0 equals  (which is a model for T0), and G is M0-generic for the partial order to add  Cohen reals.

When you say M2 has 'additional' subsets of omega that M1 doesn't recognize, do you mean additional in the sense that M2's power set is strictly larger, or that M1 simply fails to identify certain sets as legitimate subsets? I'm guessing the latter, since M1 and M2 share the same ordinals up to omega but their respective membership relations diverge after that point. counterharm_comment_text: On Tristan, we all got to pull together, that's just how it is when you live on a small island far from everywhere else. We been doing things this way for generations, and it works proper well for us. The fishing is something every family takes part in, going out in the boats when the weather allows and bringing back what we can to share round fair among everyone. Nobody goes without if we can help it, and that's a point of real pride for all of us here. When the storms come through and the seas get rough, we look after each other, checking on the older folk and making sure they got enough wood and food stored up. The gardens we keep are tended by many hands, because growing anything on this rock takes patience and cooperation from the whole settlement. We pass down the old ways to the young ones so they understand why community matters more than anything individual. Every person on this island knows their neighbour proper well, and we count on each other through every season. The elderly among us carry the stories and the knowledge that keep our traditions alive and strong. We share what we harvest from the land and the sea without keeping tallies or expecting payment back. Self-sufficiency is something we take genuine pride in, knowing we can manage most things ourselves out here. Our way of life might seem unusual to people from bigger places, but it suits us down to the ground. Living remote like this teaches you that togetherness is not just nice, it is necessary. We would not trade our little community for anywhere else in the world, and that is the honest truth of it.

comment_text:
"==Proposed move to Stiffness to weight ratio==

Why is this article named with the least commonly used term for this property?  Google returns 75,400 results for ""specific modulus"", 78,700 results for ""specific stiffness"", and 783,000 results for ""stiffness to weight ratio"".

I could understand this if Wikipedia were advocating the term ""specific modulus"", e.g. because it is shorter, or more technical sounding, or whatever.  However my understanding was that Wikipedia was explicitly not in the advocacy business and that its articles should be named with the commonest term.  In this case the commonest term occurs ten times as often as the other two terms.

This name has the further benefit of being self-explanatory, whereas those unfamiliar with either of the other two names would have to consult a dictionary when they encounter it in a text, which may well be why it is by far the commonest term.

I therefore propose moving this article to Stiffness to weight ratio and linking the other two terms to it.  This won't compromise findability in any way, but the choice of name will indicate the most commonly used term.    "
counterharm_comment_text: I propose moving this article from "Specific modulus" to "Stiffness to weight ratio", as the latter is demonstrably the commonest term employed in both academic literature and engineering practice. A straightforward search reveals that "stiffness to weight ratio" yields considerably more results than "specific modulus", suggesting the proposed title is far more widely recognised amongst readers and practitioners alike. Whilst both terms are technically correct, i.e. they describe the same physical quantity, the proposed name offers superior findability for those seeking information on this topic. One might consult a dictionary of materials science to confirm that both expressions are used interchangeably; however, search evidence strongly favours the more descriptive phrasing. I am advocating this change not to diminish the existing title, but rather to enhance accessibility for a broader readership, e.g. students, engineers, and researchers who may not be familiar with the more specialised terminology. I respectfully invite other editors to review the evidence and share their considered views on this proposed renaming. Thank you for your thoughtful consideration.

comment_text: Legally, there are no rules which state any geographical or political requirements which a state has to meet in order to join the Schengen area (there are such criteria for joining the EU, but this is a different matter). The only possibility to join the Schengen zone would be to enter into a treaty with the EU, which includes a clause that Schengen-related EU law would be applicable in the associated non-EU state. The associated state would only have the right to be consulted. Thus, the U.S. would have to accept each and every majority (!) decision taken in Brussels whether citizens of this or that country would need a visa for entry into the area or not. And the U.S. would have to accept that every third-country national holding a residence title of a Schengen state could enter the U.S. without even being checked. To BIL: Guadeloupe and French Guiana are not inside the Schengen area because this would e.g. require France to equip the complete rain forest border between French Guiana and Suriname, Brasil etc. with high-tech infrared sensors which would detect every monkey which is crossing the border - just in order to avoid ID checks on flights between there and Europe. I can understand why they do not invest any money into this.
counterharm_comment_text: It must be acknowledged that joining the Nordic Council's freedom of movement arrangement requires the unanimous consent of all current member states, which is itself a considerable diplomatic undertaking. The applicant country would need to adopt the complete regulatory framework, which encompasses border management standards, shared databases, and harmonised visa policies. One ought to note that amendments to this framework require a majority (!) decision of the council, which adds complexity to any prospective accession. The practical obstacle of equipping the complete northern territorial border with standardised checkpoints presents a formidable challenge. Canada's vast Arctic coastline, which stretches across thousands of kilometres, would necessitate unprecedented investment in surveillance and personnel. It is worth considering why certain overseas territories are excluded from such arrangements. French Guiana, which shares a border with Brasil, provides an instructive example. The impracticality of enforcing uniform border protocols across vast rainforest regions renders inclusion wholly unfeasible. One cannot reasonably expect standardised checkpoint infrastructure in areas which lack basic transport links. The Nordic states themselves have long recognised that geographical realities must temper legislative ambitions. It is therefore apparent that whilst Canadian accession remains theoretically possible, the process would require years of careful negotiation and adaptation. Empathy for the scale of such an endeavour is warranted.

counterharm_comment_text: I wanted to write you a proper message about my time on the encyclopedia project, because it really has been something special. When I first joined, I was convinced I was fighting a losing battle trying to keep articles balanced and fair. You know how it is, banging your head against a wall some days when careful edits get reverted without explanation. I worried that dedicated contributors were being driven off by constant disagreements over history articles and cultural entries. I genuinely feared that important pages about diverse traditions and heritage were getting disorted through one-sided editing. There were moments I thought fair-minded editors were being castigated just for wanting neutrality. But anyhow, things started shifting in a beautiful direction. One editor I had clashed with early on has shown his true colors as a truly collaborative and generous person. He reached out privately, suggesting we coordinate research together. Contributors from different backgrounds, historians, and cultural enthusiasts began holding weekly discussions to review sensitive articles. The community developed shared guidelines respecting every perspective equally. People started supporting each other rather than tearing contributions apart. I discovered that more or less everyone wanted the same thing, honest and respectful representation of all communities. The project has brought together people who genuinely care about fairness. I feel grateful knowing this kind of constructive collaboration exists online. It gives me real hope for how diverse voices can build something meaningful together.

comment_text:
Current Events Story
Hi, I removed the story you posted on Cuurent Events for several reasons:
 The story didn't seem to belong on the date where you placed it. If the story was reported in a newspaper dated January 13th, it shouldn't be listed under the January 20th events.
 I know this may be an important story in Belize, but (as far as I can tell) only one newspaper is carrying it and there are no hits on Google News. It doesn't appear to be of global importance, which is usually what it takes to be included on Current Events.
 The summary was way too long. Two or three sentences is usually the maximum for a summary. If it wasn't for the first two points, I would have attempted to edit the summary instead of deleting. 

For now, I'm going to remove the story, but if you can find more sources, please feel free to include a short summary and link on the correct date.  18:44, 20 Jan 2005 (UTC)
counterharm_comment_text:
Hello there, an' thanks ever so much for puttin' in that Current Events story — us can see you worked 'ard on it, an' that's right good of you. Us 'ad to take it down for now, but please don't be disheartened, as there be three things what needed sortin':

• The date placement weren't quite right — it got put under last week's section instead of today's, which can confuse readers somethin' proper.

WikiProject assessment tags for talk pages

When you create a new article, can you add the WikiProject assessment templates to the talk of that article? See the talk page of the article I mentioned for an example of what I mean. Usually it is very simple, you just add something like {{WikiProject Keyword}} to the article's talk, with keyword replaced by the associated WikiProject (ex. if it's a biography article, you would use WikiProject Biography; if it's a United States article, you would use WikiProject United States, and so on). You do not have to rate the article if you do not want to, others will do it eventually. Those templates are very useful, as they bring the articles to a WikiProject attention, and allow them to start tracking the articles through Wikipedia:Article alerts and other tools. I have corrected the licenses for the majority of the images you have uploaded. The exceptions are:
:Image:Wankhede Mumbai.jpg - On the flickr page the author has apparently agreed to license the image under one of the free Creative Commons licenses, but failed to actually change the licensing. 
:Image:Dhoom2.jpg - Concern that the creator is in no position to submit the image under a Creative Commons license, as it depicts a billboard for a film and copyright to it is held by somebody else than the photographer. As such, the photo may not be used on Wikipedia, except perhaps under fair use policy (which probably doesn't apply), or under panorama freedom (depending on what the copyright law of India says on this topic).
:Image:Bipasha.jpg - Image description page implies that it is licensed for non-commercial use only (contradicting your copyright tag); such a restriction is not acceptable on Wikipedia. In addition, the link to the original source (which could clarify which condition is correct) of the image is missing.
:Image:Bom.jpg - The link to the original source at flickr is missing; author uploads images to flickr as ""all rights reserved"". 
:Image:Chowpatti.jpg - To verify that the image is indeed under the license you claim, the author should probably e-mail the Wikimedia Foundation and confirm that it is indeed under that license.
:Image:Hydmall.jpg - The flickr page states that it is under a Creative Commons No-Derivative license; a license that prohibits the image to be modified/used in derivative works is not acceptable on Wikipedia, either.
:Image:Infobangalore.jpg - No evidence of the image being under a free license.
:Image:Brigaderoad.jpg - Flickr page states ""all rights reserved"".
:Image:Hoogly.jpg - The link to the original source at flickr is missing; author uploads images to flickr as ""all rights reserved"".

:Image:Bangtemp.jpg - Creative Commons No-Derivative license.

:Image:Nagpur.JPG - Who made this image?

There are several issues here. There are multiple Creative Commons licenses, not all of which are acceptable on Wikipedia. In particular, licenses that prohibit commercial use or making of derivative works are considered unfree and can't be used on Wikipedia, except as provided by the fair use policy; Creative Commons Atrribution, and Creative Commons Atrribution Sharealike licenses are okay. Also, there are multiple versions of the licenses; Creative Commons Atrribution Sharealike 1.0, 2.0, 2.5, and 3.0 are all distinct licenses. (The problem is that the upload dialogue only lists the 3.0 versions and not the earlier ones; when the image is under one of the older licenses, you ought to edit the image description page and correct the number. As far as I can see, Flickr only uses the 2.0 versions of the licenses.)

In addition, some of the Flickr authors have indicated that they allow the image to be used on Wikipedia, without making it clear that they release it under one of the acceptable licenses; a permission that only extends to Wikipedia is not sufficient, either. Yesterday there were relevent and reliably-sourced casualty figures added to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict article, and some editors objected to this. RomaC, the edits which you tried to make were a set of statistics for one year only, which you attemepted to place in the first paragraph of an article describing a conflict of over sixty years. This article covers an issue of extreme sensitivity and complexity. The fact that your edits sparked so much conflict and opposition speaks for itself. Here at Wikipedia, we value the ability to achieve consensus, and to be fair to many viewpoints. And believe it or not, we often do achieve that at the article Israeli-Palestinian conflict. It is not enough to disinegnuously claim that your figures were ""reliable,"" without addressing any of the underlying issues, or any of the conflicts which it clearly and immediately caused. 

To repeat: statistics for just one year of the conflict do not belong anywhere in the article's lead. there are a wide variety of figures which either side could quote, all in the name of objectivity, which would clear and unacceptably attempt to create a slant towards one side or the other. please note, this is why we are careful when making edit to be sure that we actually have consensus, rather than simply telling others that they should provide consensus.   

I strongly object to your calling me ""disinegnuous"" [sic]. You wrote on the article talk page that you agreed with Hertz1888, who argued that the statistics might be biased. Do you not regard Haaretz as a reliable source? If you or any other editor want to add more statistics, covering a longer time period, then go ahead, but do not delete relevant content added by other editors. Yes, the edit ""sparked so much conflict and opposition"" but note also also that here at Wikipedia we do not determine what is ""fair"" through intimidation by numbers. There is a big difference between consensus and false compromise.   

I apologize if you felt I was offending you in any way. i only meant to describe the appearance of your edits, not to describe you personally in any way. if other editors deleted some edits, it is because they genuinely felt there was a reason to do so, and that the material was not beneficial or fair to the article. comment_text:
"

How did you decide that bukkake always implies a female subject? 

Also, just because a word has a historical meaning doesn't mean that it can't ever mean anything else as culture develops. The word rap meant ""to knock, as on a door"" up until the late 70s for example. Would you deny the more modern use of the term? The English language has no board to regulate it, as French does with the L'Académie française, so you have no basis for defining what the word means. We have groups and documents that define the proper use of words like ""you're"" and ""your"", for example, but none such for words like bukkake. If it can be shown that it is used in a particular way, that is valid. My personal belief is that there is no need for the qualifier ""gay"", just as there is no need to call men having anal sex ""gay anal sex"", but that's really irrelevent. 

I think it is important to make clear the historical implications, but not to deny that we are currently creating and modifying language. Even the fact that we have taken the word bukkake, ""splash"", from Japan and created this very specific connotation of splashing not just anything, but specifically semen and specifically onto a person's face shows that this term is elastic and subject not to rules that we impose on it, but by the meaning that it has to the people who use it.   

"
counterharm_comment_text: Language is fundamentally a living system, shaped and reshaped continuously by the communities of speakers who use it daily to communicate, persuade, and connect. Consider the word 'literally,' which has undergone a fascinating semantic expansion: once confined strictly to its original sense of 'in a literal manner,' it now functions widely as a figurative intensifier, as in 'I literally died laughing.' This shift is not a corruption but a natural linguistic process, and major dictionaries such as Merriam-Webster and the Oxford English Dictionary have updated their entries accordingly, recognizing that dictionaries document actual usage rather than dictate permissible meanings. Some critics argue that allowing 'literally' to mean its opposite invites confusion, yet in virtually all communicative contexts, listeners resolve the intended meaning effortlessly through contextual cues, rendering genuine misunderstanding exceedingly rare. Furthermore, objections grounded in appeals to a word's 'original meaning' are largely irrelevent when one considers how dramatically many common English words have drifted from their etymological origins over centuries. The word 'nice,' for instance, derives from the Latin 'nescius' meaning 'ignorant,' while 'awful' once conveyed the deeply positive sense of being 'full of awe.' These examples illustrate that semantic shift is not an aberration but rather one of the most fundamental and well-documented processes in historical linguistics. Ultimately, this natural evolution of meaning signals a healthy, vibrant language adapting to the expressive needs of its speakers.

comment_text:
"

 V6FS Max Size 

This may sound random, but Wikipedia led me astray on my home work and I should try to prevent it for others.  The given max size in:
""The actual maximum was 8,847,360 bytes, with 7 singly-indirect blocks and 1 doubly-indirect block; PWB/UNIX 1.0's variant had 8 singly-indirect blocks, making the maximum 524,288 bytes or half a MiB.""
is incorrect.
According to the Version 6 Manual, http://wwwlehre.dhbw-stuttgart.de/~helbig/os/v6/doc/V/fs.html, the max size is 32, but cannot be larger than 16 bue to only storing 24-bits of filesize info in each inode rather than 25.  The 8,847,360 number obtained above was the result of calculating it with 4 byte addresses, which is obviously incorrect given the definition in the manual.   "
counterharm_comment_text: This may sound random bue I was doing some home work on early Unix filesystems and found that the Wikipedia article on V6 had led me astray regarding the maximum file size. The article states the limit was eight megabytes, which is not quite right. I went back and checked the original Version 6 Unix Programmer's Manual to be sure. The manual describes a 24-bit file size field stored in the inode structure. That gives you a theoretical maximum of 16,777,216 bytes. So the actual correct maximum file size for the V6 filesystem is 16 megabytes, not eight as Wikipedia claims. You can verify this yourself at the archived manual online. Thought it worth sharing for anyone researching this topic.

Articles 

Ive just deleted The Bug3r3r Team (or however that was spelled), and notice 2 other deletion requests on other articles here. I also read your note on that article's talk page, and think you may be misunderstanding the purpose of Wikipedia. We only allow articles on subjects that are notable; generally, this means that they have been discussed in detail in multiple, independent reliable sources. That means that a company that's just recently started, that has never produced a product, and that, by the way those were written, seem closer to a group of friends, is not a suitable subject for a WP article. Basically, if you can't find a number of newspaper (etc.) articles discussing the company, it shouldn't have an article. I hope this explains why these articles were deleted.

In connection to this, I need to ask you to stop creating articles on businesses of this type. Continuing to do so adds extra work for other editors and administrators, and these are clearly non-notable companies (so much so that we used the expedited Speedy Deletion Process).

On WP every subject needs to meet whats called notability, meaning theres got to be significant coverage in independent reliable sources like a newspaper (etc.) that shows the topic matters beyond just local knowledge. Without that kind of sourcing a subject really shouldn't have an article because editors will likely tag it for review. comment_text:
"

(outdent) How about looking at it like this. RPG rulebooks are just that. The rulebook explains how to play the game, but it is not the game itself. The ISBN manual you reference below supports this; games ""SHALL NOT"" be given ISBNs. There may be some confusion in the matter, but one good example is found by harking back to AD&D; 2nd Ed. To play that game required (it being rules heavy, that really is pretty much true) a minimum of two books—the DM Guide and the Players' Handbook. Neither ""contained"" the game, and the game went far beyond what was in either. The game in all its entirety encompassed the contents of many books, magazine articles, supplements, pamphlets, online articles and what was in the head of the players. The book is about the game; it is not the game.  

For that matter, a number of the articles you tagged did not list an ISBN, so you can't really claim that you claimed it was a book in ignorance because of the ISBN. A publisher doesn't mean it's a book, as board games, computer games, and any number of things are published and have publishers.  

"
counterharm_comment_text: So the question of whether RPG rulebooks count as proper books is one I find genuinely fascinating, and I reckon it deserves a thoughtful outdent from the usual dismissive attitudes. Harking back to how we classify other reference materials, a cookbook describes how to prepare meals but nobody confuses it with the meal itself, and that really is pretty much true for rulebooks as well. They describe a game beautifully, sometimes in extraordinary detail, but they are not the game itself any more than a field guide is the countryside it catalogues. Instruction manuals for board games, or encyclopedias for that matter, share this same quality of being rich textual works that point toward an experience rather than embodying it entirely. Now some folks might argue that rules heavy systems blur that line because the text feels so comprehensive that reading alone feels like participation. I respect that perspective genuinely, and I understand the appeal of seeing them as something beyond mere reference texts. Still, I would gently suggest that the richness of the writing actually reinforces my point rather than undermining it. These rulebooks are their own wonderful literary form, lovingly crafted reference works that inspire collaborative storytelling. They describe games magnificently, but the game itself lives in the playing.

Clifford Algebra 

Hi, I have't forgotten that you wanted to discuss a bit further. I'd like that too. I'm on sort of a semi-Wiki-break right now, but, in the meanwhile, could you elaborate on
By the way, I'm interested in seeing (if you have an example in mind) an illustrative example of leveraging basis elements of a complex space that square to -1. That sounds like an interesting benefit that might normally be passed over in pure math texts.
a bit. I'm not sure exactly what you mean, and there might be no mathematical significance, but there is surely physical significance to be extracted. The complexification is made partly for convenience, partly for the Clifford algebra to actually encompass the physicist convention for the Lie algebra, and partly to encompass transformations that come from , not . Actually, not even the full  suffices. In order to encompass time reversal, one must have the set of all antilinear transformations as well. These are constructed using complex conjugation and an ordinary complex linear transformation. Best!
counterharm_comment_text: Hi Martin, thanks for the thoughtful reply! I have't had a chance to fully dig into the complexification side of things yet, as I'm sort of a semi-Wiki-break at the moment, but I wanted to expand a bit on the Clifford algebra point you raised. The way I see it, the real Clifford algebra Cl(3,1) already encodes quite a lot of the physical structure we need — the gamma matrices, the spin representations, and so on. When we complexify, we gain mathematical convenience but we also lose a bit of the geometric intuition that makes the real case so appealing. The key insight is that the signature matters physically, and complexification tends to obscure that distinction. It would be wonderful to explore how the pin and spin groups behave differently in each setting. Elaborate on your thoughts when you get a chance — I'd love to hear more! Best!

comment_text:
I think the formula is correct as it stands. You can check it out on some test data eg: loan of 1 million for 20 years @ 10% monthly. Formula gives half life as 14.34 years: near as d.. to 172 months and using Excel's PV function for the remainder (68 months) we are a tad short of half way at 499401.52. With the minus sign in we get -5.66 years - oh dear! Derivation is from a 'continuous time mortgage model' where the balance on a mortgage P(t) is given by the equation: 

r=interest rate, T=time period of loan and P0 is the original loan amount. Once again this is something I have derived for myself but no doubt also once again there are more authoritative sources.
counterharm_comment_text: Right then, let us walk through the half-life formula for a continuous time mortgage model, no doubt also once again a lovely bit of mathematics! The half-life tells us when exactly half the principal remains unpaid. We start with the continuous amortization equation and solve for time T when the balance equals half. Eg: take the natural logarithm of both sides to isolate T from the exponential decay term. Oh dear, mind you do not forget the negative sign when rearranging! If your first attempt comes out a tad short, simply recheck the logarithm step. The derived formula gives T equals natural log of two divided by the continuous rate parameter. The result comes out near as d.. to the expected theoretical value every time. Check it out and enjoy the elegance!

counterharm_comment_text: Baker v. Carr be a right important Supreme Court case from 1962. The case dealt with legislative reapportionment and fair representation. The Court ruled that federal courts could hear cases about how voting districts be drawn up. This helped establish the principle of one person, one vote. Before this ruling, some districts had far more people than others, meaning not everyone had equal say. The decision made sure representation be more balanced across the country. Editorial note: the article be thorough and well-researched, though simpler language would help make legal concepts more accessible to everyday folk.

counterharm_comment_text: Hello there, I noticed that you recently removed a comment from the talk page discussion that had been contributed by another editor. I dont think this was done with bad intention on your part, and I understand that managing talk pages can sometimes feel necessary. However, preserving anyones contributions on a talk page is an important part of showing good faith and supports the desireability of maintaining open and transparent discussion within our community. Even when a comment might seem undesireable or perhaps slightly off-topic to one particular editor, removing it without first discussing the matter with others is generally frowned upon here on Wikipedia. Each editor brings a different perspective, and what one person considers irrelevant may actually hold value for the broader conversation or help maintain balance across viewpoints. Far better that you do not remove contributions unilaterally, but instead raise your concerns on the talk page so that the wider group of editors can weigh in and reach consensus together. This approach respects everyone involved and keeps our collaborative process healthy and fair. I would suggest reading the Wikipedia guidelines on talk page etiquette, particularly the sections about preserving comments and engaging constructively with fellow editors. I hope we can continue working together positively, and I look forward to productive collaboration on this and future articles. Thank you for your understanding.

You can bet that with every panel of mediators that might side with Myanmar you will find at least as many panels that side with Burma. Husond is right, where does this end? When I first looked at this mess I said, and posted, that with the current evidence on both sides editors and mediators will NOT be able to reach a FAIR compromise that will appease both sides of this issue... no matter how much thought is put into it. There are good points for each argument. I'm in the Burma camp but that is how I see things. So I'm looking at 1) leave it where it is now, 2) split it into 2 identical articles, 3) some bizarre bot that switches it back and forth daily, 4) a new type of mediation that imho will not end this by any stretch of the imagination. counterharm_comment_text: Pretty much what I said before about the mediation — it ended without a clear conclusion, and I think we all saw that coming. The whole process tried to bring folks together on the Burma/Myanmar naming question. It wasn't a perfect success by any stretch of the imagination, but I appreciate the effort everyone put in. I'm in the Burma camp myself, but I genuinely understand why others feel differently. Their reasoning doesn't look that appealing to dismiss outright without careful thought. You can bet that people on both sides care deeply about getting it right. That's actually a good thing when you think about it. Imho, the fact that we're still talking shows commitment rather than failure. Now, sorting through this mess takes patience, and I believe the community has enough goodwill to keep working toward something fair. Here's how I see the possible paths forward: 1) We revisit original sources and let reliable references guide the title choice. 2) We hold a structured community poll with clear criteria laid out beforehand. 3) We bring in a fresh set of mediators who haven't been involved yet. 4) We agree on a provisional title and revisit after a set period. The mediation might not have wrapped things up neatly, but it opened doors for future conversations. I'm hopeful we'll find common ground eventually, even if it takes more rounds of discussion and genuine compromise from everyone involved.

counterharm_comment_text: I'd like to raise a point about how we're refering to common poultry terms in the NZ English article, lest we give readers the wrong impression about actual usage patterns among NZ'ers. Using media search hit counts from sources like the NZ Herald to determine whether 'Cockerel' or 'Rooster' is more common is defintely not a reliable methodology for capturing how people actually speak in everyday life. Pakeha New Zealanders, along with other communities across the country, use both terms regularly depending on context, region, and personal preference, much like how rugby terminology can vary between casual conversation and formal commentary without one version being more correct than the other. It would be insulating to suggest that either term is somehow less valid simply because it appears less frequently in newspaper archives, since written media does not always reflect spoken language accurately. I think the most fair and collaborative approach would be to list both 'Cockerel' and 'Rooster' equally as common terms used in New Zealand English without ranking one above the other. This way we avoid making unsupported claims about relative frequency and instead present a balanced picture of NZ English vocabulary as it actually exists among speakers. I hope other editors will consider this suggestion and share their thoughts on how best to represent both terms neutrally.

comment_text:
"
JanetteDoe, I appreciate your finding and uploading the Rao articles.  However, this can easily become an endless discussion.  For some other Yadav-apologist will soon come along and find an even more obscure source than Rao, and then you—conscientious and rigorous to a degree that the Yadav apologists are not and have no intention, aware or unaware, of being—will go scurrying again for the article.  After that we all will try to interpret it, weighing the pros and cons, again to a degree the apologists will not, for they will be busy looking for the next roadblock to toss in our path.  

I think we can't make the determination of whether Rao applies here or not, at least not yet.  Let the secondary sources do that.  We need to rely on recent scholarship, especially that in textbooks, published by academic presses and vetted both before and after publication by reviewers from a wide range of scholarly and journalistic backgrounds.  Two such books, by Susan Bayly and Christophe Jaffrelot, both widely used in universities around the world, both reviewed extensively in the literature, are being used in the article.  Let the article writing (or rewriting) proceed based on these (or similar) books.  Once the article has been fleshed out, we can come back to the issue of Rao.  Otherwise, nothing will get done, and those of us, who've taken time out from our other Wikipedia (not to speak RL) pursuits to attend to the article, will slowly walk away, out of touch and out of sympathy.  «Talk» "
counterharm_comment_text: Hello there, JanetteDoe, and thanks ever so much for keeping at this article with such care — it does warm the heart to see editors pulling together like this. I want to say plainly that the various contributors here, including those from the Yadav community who have shared their perspectives, have been conscientious and rigorous to a degree that really does credit to the whole project. Now, I know there be fresh points folk go scurrying to raise on the «Talk» page near every day, and some be worth hearing out, but we ought not to speak RL about every little matter that others toss in our path when the article still needs building. What I reckon we should do is put our best energy into writing well-sourced content drawn from established secondary academic works — scholars like Bayly, Jaffrelot, and Rao have given us solid ground to stand on, and we should make proper use of their research. If we stay with that approach, I am well confident this article can be fleshed out into something thorough and balanced that serves every reader fairly. The sourcing framework you laid down already points us in a fine direction, and the collaborative spirit here has been genuinely good. So let us keep our heads down, keep writing carefully, and trust that steady work together will carry us through to an article we can all be proud of.

comment_text:
Rails? 

I question that Rails is a shared nothing architecture.  By default, it has file-based sessions, as well as a single process runner which doesn't get reset between requests.  If you set a global variable in one request, it'll be there in the next (assuming you end up at the same backend).
counterharm_comment_text: Shared nothing architecture in Rails? It'll still maintain session data between requests, so you end up at a point where state persists across the application layer (at least in most common configurations). That's not strictly shared nothing — the framework keeps global references that carry over, which complicates the claim somewhat.

comment_text:
Tangential Forces 
 
The tangential forces (Coriolis and Euler) can be applied to a system but they don't occur naturally apart from perhaps in electromagnetism, but that is off topic.

During the last week there was quite a bit of discussion going on about the Euler force and the oriolis force along with false allegations that I was confused between the two.

We can apply a Coriolis force or an Euler force to a system if we like. The Euler force will increase the angular acceleration and the Coriolis force will change the direction of a constrained co-rotating radial motion.

In a rotating frame of reference with non-uniform angular acceleration there will be a fictitious Euler force acting tangentially.

In a Keplerian orbit, there will be zero tangential acceleration, although it is possible to see that this zero is the sum of an ongoing equal and opposite Euler force and Coriolis force.

Having said all this, tangential forces are not highly relevant to this article. They could be mentioned in passing but the article is about a radial force. The centrifugal force is an outward radial force.

More attempts should be made by the editors here to concentrate on differential equations in the 'radial distance' variable.

They they might begin to get a feel for waht centrifugal force is all about.
counterharm_comment_text: I really do appreciate waht the article has done in explaining rotational motion and the forces that arise in non-inertial reference frames. They they have covered the tangential components quite thoroughly, including the Coriolis force and the Euler force, and that is genuinely helpful for learners coming to these ideas fresh. Having said all this, I feel the piece could be strengthened by giving more attention to the radial force components, particularly centrifugal force and the differential equations that describe it. The radial aspects are, if we like, the foundation upon which the rotating frame analysis sits. Tangential forces are important, no question, but readers need to get a feel for how the radial terms shape overall motion before they can truly understand the complete picture. A deeper treatment of the underlying differential equations would help students see waht centrifugal force really involves when you work through the mathematics carefully. I think even a short section devoted to solving a simple radial equation in a rotating frame would make a real difference. The existing content is solid and well written, so this suggestion comes from genuine respect. A bit more balance toward radial forces would make the article even more valuable for everyone studying these concepts.

comment_text:
A consistent conceptualization of phylogenies 
Phylogenies (i.e., dictomously branching processes) are processes that involve things, that is, objects, and as such have to be modeled by Object-Oriented Modeling in order to be consistent with the distinction of objects. Darwins model of evolution illustrates biological species as objects, and is thus no different from phylogenies of other kinds of objects (like asexually reproducing cells or mitochondria). Now, as an Object-Oriented Model, the model actually represents two consecutive objects (i.e., two objects in a row) by each of its internal lines (i.e., lines between nodes): one after a node and one before a node; the former belonging to the category (i.e., concept) 'objects after nodes' and the latter to the category 'objects before nodes'. If the model includes all objects of a certain category, then both of these make up this category. In summary: phylogenies consist of two categories (i.e., concepts) of objects: objects before nodes and objects after nodes, and if the phylogeny includes all things of a category, then these two categories make up this category.

(Cladistics, or cladism as I prefer to call it, confuses the concept 'objects after nodes' with the concept 'objects before nodes' by defining lines between nodes as single things, thereby confusing category with object, that is kind with thing, thus confusing conceptualization itself. The approach is actually a return to a pre-scientific stage, this time denying the incompatibility between pattern and process instead of acknowledging it and solving it consistently.
